name: TV Series Drama (Streaming Format)
description: Multi-episode drama structure for streaming platforms. No ad breaks. 45-65 minute episodes with season arc. Cliffhanger formulas and A/B/C plot weaving.

beats.name: Cold Open / Hook
beats.description: Start mid-action or with a provocative scene that hooks the viewer. Can be a flash-forward, a mystery moment, or an action peak. In streaming, this replaces the need for a traditional teaser.
beats.purpose: Prevent the viewer from clicking away. You have 30 seconds to hook them.

requiredElements.description: Immediate hook — action, mystery, or provocative moment

beats.name: Title Sequence / Transition
beats.description: Brief title card or opening credits. In streaming, this is often short (15-30 seconds) or skippable.
beats.purpose: Brand the show. Establish visual identity.

beats.name: Status Quo Update / Where We Left Off
beats.description: Reestablish where each plot thread stands. Remind the viewer of the ongoing conflicts. This is NOT recap — it's the characters dealing with the aftermath of the previous episode.
beats.purpose: Orient returning viewers without repeating exposition.

requiredElements.description: Touch each active plot thread briefly
For early episodes: introduce new characters

beats.name: Episode Catalyst / This Episode's Problem
beats.description: A new development that creates this episode's central question. This is on top of the season arc — it's the specific problem of THIS episode.
beats.purpose: Give the episode its own identity and question that can be partially resolved.

requiredElements.description: New development that drives this episode's A-plot

beats.name: Rising Action — A Plot
beats.description: The A-plot advances. Characters take action on the episode's central problem. Interweaved with B and C plot scenes.
beats.purpose: Drive the main story forward. Deepen character relationships.

beats.name: B-Plot Development / Subplot Deepening
beats.description: The B-plot (and C-plot if present) gets significant screen time. These subplots should contrast or mirror the A-plot thematically.
beats.purpose: Provide variety, deepen world-building, explore theme from different angles.

beats.name: Midpoint Twist / Major Revelation
beats.description: A significant revelation or reversal at the episode's midpoint. Changes the direction of the A-plot. In serialized dramas, this often connects to the larger season arc.
beats.purpose: Re-engage the viewer. Shift the dynamic. Often the episode's most memorable moment.

requiredElements.description: Major twist or revelation that reframes the episode

beats.name: Complications / Plot Convergence
beats.description: A and B plots begin to intersect. Complications from one plot affect the other. Characters face harder choices. The season arc advances.
beats.purpose: Weave plotlines together. Raise all stakes simultaneously.

requiredElements.description: Plots collide — action in one affects the other
A character faces a difficult moral choice

beats.name: Episode Crisis / Low Point
beats.description: The episode's darkest moment. The A-plot problem seems unsolvable. A relationship fractures. A plan fails.
beats.purpose: Hit the emotional bottom before the episode's resolution.

requiredElements.description: Character's lowest point in this episode

beats.name: Episode Climax / Partial Resolution
beats.description: The episode's A-plot question is answered (or partially answered). This episode's specific problem is resolved, but the season arc advances or complicates.
beats.purpose: Satisfy the episode while driving the season forward.

requiredElements.description: Climactic confrontation resolving this episode's main conflict

beats.name: Fallout / New Status Quo
beats.description: Characters deal with the consequences of the climax. New relationships, new dynamics. Seeds planted for the next episode.
beats.purpose: Transition to new status quo. Show consequences. Plant next-episode hooks.

beats.name: Cliffhanger / End Hook
beats.description: A final revelation, threat, or question that makes the viewer NEED to watch the next episode. Not every episode needs a cliffhanger, but most should end on a strong hook.
beats.purpose: Drive binge-watching. Make the 'Next Episode' button irresistible.

requiredElements.description: Final hook — new threat, twist, or unanswered question

genreRules.rule: Hook the viewer within 30 seconds — no slow builds for streaming episode openers
Weave A/B/C plots: never go more than 8 minutes without switching plot threads
Screen time allocation: A-plot 50-60%, B-plot 25-35%, C-plot 10-15%
End every episode on a hook — cliffhanger, revelation, or unresolved tension
Season arc checkpoints: major advancement at episodes 1, 3-4, 5-6 (midseason), 8-9, finale
Character relationships must evolve episode-to-episode — never static
One 'watercooler moment' per episode — the scene people talk about

mainPlot.name: Season Arc
mainPlot.description: The overarching question/conflict that drives the entire season
mainPlot.thematicFunction: The macro-level dramatic question: will the protagonist achieve their season-long goal?

subPlots.name: Episode A-Plot
subPlots.description: This episode's specific problem/question, connected to but distinct from the season arc
subPlots.thematicFunction: Provides episodic satisfaction while advancing the season

subPlots.name: Episode B-Plot
subPlots.description: Secondary story exploring theme from a different angle or different characters

subPlots.name: Episode C-Plot (Runner)
subPlots.description: Light subplot providing tonal variety or comic relief
subPlots.thematicFunction: Pacing relief, world-building, or setup for future episodes

phases.name: New Challenge
phases.internalConflict: This challenge touches on season-arc weakness

phases.name: Crisis Point
phases.internalConflict: Must make a choice that reveals character

phases.name: Episode Resolution
phases.internalConflict: One step closer to (or further from) season-arc transformation
phases.externalManifest: New status quo — slightly different person than episode start
